title: High Dimensional Variants of the Finite Field Kakeya Problem
speaker: Zeev Dvir
abstract: The finite field Kakeya problem asks about the size of the smallest set in (F_q)^n containing a line in every direction.  Raised by Wolff in 1999 as a ‘toy’ version of the Euclidean Kakeya conjecture, this problem is now completely resolved using the polynomial method. In this talk I will describe recent progress on its higher dimensional variant in which lines are replaced with k-dimensional flats.

proof_stages.label: Classical finite-field Kakeya setup and sharp line problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Kakeya sets in F_q^n as sets containing a line in every direction, recalls Wolff's size question, states the known lower bound of order q^n with sharp constant, and gives the sharpness construction as background for the higher-dimensional variants.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial-method proof and its limitation
proof_stages.summary: The classical lower bound is explained by interpolating a low-degree polynomial vanishing on a too-small Kakeya set, forcing its top homogeneous part to vanish in every direction, while the same argument is shown to fail for sparse intersections with higher-dimensional flats.

proof_stages.label: Higher-dimensional Kakeya and Furstenberg definitions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines d-Kakeya sets and then (d,m)-Furstenberg sets, states the near-full-density theorem for true d-Kakeya sets and the older m^{n/d} lower bound for Furstenberg sets and schemes, including the role of scheme-theoretic sharpness.

proof_stages.label: Set-theoretic near-full-size Furstenberg theorem
proof_stages.summary: For d at least 2 and m not too small, the newer theorem gives a much stronger lower bound close to q^n for finite sets, and its sharpness is justified by the averaging construction giving (d,m)-Furstenberg sets of size m q^{n-d}.

proof_stages.label: Layered incidence-graph expansion mechanism
proof_stages.summary: For d=2 the proof is recast in a point-line-plane incidence graph, where the one-dimensional Kakeya theorem becomes an expansion statement and can be shifted upward to show that one plane in every direction contains a large fraction of all lines.

proof_stages.label: Rich-flat sampling reduction to rich lines
proof_stages.summary: An m-rich plane contains, by pairwise-independent sampling over its lines, many roughly m/q-rich lines, and the incidence expansion then produces too many rich lines unless the original set has the asserted size; the same reasoning is noted to work symmetrically for poor flats.

proof_stages.label: Balanced-flat theorem
proof_stages.summary: Rich and poor flats are unified into the notion of epsilon-balanced affine flats, leading to a stronger theorem: for suitable parameters, most d-dimensional directions have all affine shifts balanced with respect to the set.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and external applications
proof_stages.summary: The balanced-slicing theorem is translated into consequences for linear hashing and min-entropy extraction, sketched in a lattice-covering application, and compared in questions with real and p-adic Kakeya phenomena.

examples.name: Leftover hash lemma and min-entropy extraction
examples.note: The leftover hash lemma is used as an application framework: balanced slices of a set correspond to nearly uniform outputs of a random linear map, yielding an L-infinity analogue for flat sources.
examples.engagement_reason: He states the leftover hash lemma, unpacks min-entropy and random linear maps, sketches the collision-probability proof idea, and derives the balanced-slicing corollary for near-uniform extraction.
